summary: Big day for Reynoldstown with new townhomes finally breaking ground after a five-year wait, while Inman Park gets a fresh Asian-American comfort food spot called Mushi Ni. Meanwhile, TSA chaos at Hartsfield has ICE agents wandering around mostly being unhelpful.

topStory.headline: Reynoldstown Beltline Corner Finally Gets Its Townhomes
topStory.summary: After sitting empty for five years, that cleared lot at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace is finally seeing construction. Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is building 85 residential units about a block from the Eastside Trail. Construction just started on what they're calling Camber Crossing.
topStory.body:
You know that vacant corner near the Beltline in Reynoldstown that's been an eyesore since 2021? The one where they cleared everything and then... nothing happened for years? Well, finally some good news. Embry Development has broken ground on 85 townhomes and condos at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace, just a block west of the Eastside Trail.

This project has been through more hands than a game of hot potato. Toll Brothers originally had it permitted, but Embry swooped in two years ago and took over the whole thing. They're calling it Camber Crossing now, and honestly, anything is better than that empty lot that's been collecting weeds and broken dreams. The location is prime real estate, about a block north of Memorial Drive and walking distance to all the Beltline action.

For Reynoldstown, this is exactly the kind of thoughtful infill development the neighborhood needs. It's not some massive tower that overwhelms the area, just human-scale housing that puts more feet on the Beltline. Plus, it means one less vacant lot for people to dump their old couches in.
topStory.imageAlt: Construction site with cleared ground at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown

Embry Development took over from Toll Brothers and is building 85 residential units about a block from the Eastside Trail. Construction just started on what they're calling Camber Crossing.

Well, finally some good news. Embry Development has broken ground on 85 townhomes and condos at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace, just a block west of the Eastside Trail.

This project has been through more hands than a game of hot potato. Toll Brothers originally had it permitted, but Embry swooped in two years ago and took over the whole thing. They're calling it Camber Crossing now, and honestly, anything is better than that empty lot that's been collecting weeds and broken dreams. The location is prime real estate, about a block north of Memorial Drive and walking distance to all the Beltline action.

stories.imageAlt: Construction site with cleared ground at Chester Avenue and Fulton Terrace in Reynoldstown

stories.headline: Decatur Gets Affordable Village-Style Housing That's Actually Affordable
stories.summary: Cottages at Midway is opening next week with 18 standalone houses built around shared courtyards near downtown Decatur. With median home values over $700k in Decatur, this mixed-income community on Midway Road is trying to bring back missing middle housing that regular folks can afford.
stories.body:
Finally, someone in Decatur is building housing that doesn't require a trust fund to afford. Cottages at Midway is soft-launching next week with 18 village-style houses on Midway Road, designed around shared courtyards and green space. The goal is to offer an alternative to Decatur's median home price of over $700,000, which has basically priced out everyone except tech bros and inherited wealth kids.

The 2.1-acre development sits on two formerly underused lots and represents exactly the kind of missing middle housing we need more of. Instead of cramming in luxury towers or McMansions, the developers went with human-scale architecture that actually fits the neighborhood character. The shared courtyard concept encourages the kind of front-porch community that made Decatur special in the first place.

This is smart infill development done right. It's near major commercial corridors, walkable to downtown Decatur, and designed to feel like a natural extension of the surrounding area rather than some alien spaceship that landed from the suburbs.
stories.imageAlt: Site plan showing 18 cottages arranged around shared courtyards at Cottages at Midway in Decatur

stories.headline: West End Gets Rare For-Sale Townhomes Along the Beltline
stories.summary: GW Parkside is taking shape on Hopkins Street with 23 new townhomes, with the first seven units nearing completion. Ackerman & Co. is behind this project, the same folks who own Lee + White. It's bringing much-needed for-sale housing to a popular Beltline stretch in Southwest Atlanta.
stories.body:
The West End is finally getting some love with new for-sale townhomes rising along the Beltline. GW Parkside (formerly Hopkins and White Townhomes) is taking shape at 600 Hopkins Street, with 23 units planned and the first seven nearing completion. Ackerman & Co., who also own the Lee + White complex, started this project a year ago after clearing the old buildings.

What makes this project special is that it's bringing for-sale housing to a stretch of the Beltline that's been mostly rental-heavy. The West End has seen plenty of development in recent years, but most of it has been apartments. Having actual ownership opportunities means people can put down roots instead of just passing through on their way to the next hot neighborhood.

The timing couldn't be better. The Lee + White area has become a legitimate dining and office destination, and the Beltline connection means easy access to the rest of the trail system. For the West End, this represents the kind of thoughtful development that builds on existing assets rather than trying to completely reinvent the neighborhood.
stories.imageAlt: Rendering of GW Parkside townhomes under construction in West End near Beltline

stories.headline: Mushi Ni Brings Asian-American Comfort Food to Inman Park
stories.summary: Chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez just opened their first restaurant on Elizabeth Street, right off the Freedom Park Trail. The menu features soft shell crab bao, Peking chicken platters, and Thai tea martinis. Both chefs have serious restaurant pedigree, with Jimenez having worked for Gordon Ramsay in NYC.
stories.body:
Inman Park just got a serious upgrade with Mushi Ni opening today on Elizabeth Street, just steps from the Freedom Park Trail. This is the first restaurant from chefs Michael Le and Tanya Jimenez, who've been testing their Asian-American comfort food concept as a pop-up around town. Now they've got a permanent home in one of the best food neighborhoods in Atlanta.

The menu is exactly what you want from a chef-driven casual spot. Think soft shell crab bao, chicken longanisa bao, umami fries, and crab Rangoon puffs for snacking, plus Peking chicken platters that feed two people for just $25. They're doing lunch and dinner with cocktails, including Thai tea martinis that sound perfect for the Freedom Park crowd.

Both chefs bring serious credentials to the table. Jimenez worked for Gordon Ramsay in New York before moving to Atlanta, and you can tell they've put real thought into creating food that's both elevated and approachable. The location is perfect too, catching foot traffic from the trail and the Elizabeth Street corridor. This feels like the kind of neighborhood spot that'll become a regular rotation restaurant for Inman Park locals.
stories.imageAlt: Peking chicken platter from Mushi Ni restaurant

stories.headline: Lewis Barbecue Brings Austin-Style Brisket to Ansley Mall
stories.summary: The new smokehouse behind The Cook's Warehouse has six 22-foot smokers running 24/7 in a loft with panoramic windows overlooking the Beltline bridge. Pitmaster John Lewis helped build the smokers from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they're turning out some of the best brisket in Atlanta.
stories.body:
Lewis Barbecue just opened at Ansley Mall, and let me tell you, this isn't your typical strip mall barbecue joint. John Lewis, the pitmaster behind this operation, has created something special with six massive 22-foot smokers housed in a loft space with panoramic windows overlooking the new pedestrian bridge to the Beltline. It looks more like the world's fanciest engine room than a traditional smokehouse.

Lewis helped weld these smokers himself from 1,000-gallon propane tanks, and they run 24 hours a day in staggered eight-hour shifts. The result is some of the best brisket you'll find in Atlanta, with that perfect Austin-style bark and smoke ring. The location behind The Cook's Warehouse is perfect for grabbing supplies and then walking over for lunch, or taking your food to go and eating it on the Beltline.

This represents a serious upgrade to the food options around the Ansley Mall area. Having a legitimate barbecue destination this close to Midtown and the Beltline fills a real gap in the market. Plus, watching the smoking operation through those big windows is half the entertainment.
stories.imageAlt: Loaded barbecue platter from Lewis Barbecue

stories.headline: Eastside Gets New Food Hall from NYC's Gansevoort Market Creator
stories.summary: Robert Montwaid, who created New York's famous Gansevoort Market, is opening Vela Park Food Works near East Lake Golf Club in mid-April. The food hall will feature six chef-driven concepts with craft cocktails and outdoor dining at the new Vela Park rental community.
stories.body:
The eastside is about to get a serious food destination with Vela Park Food Works opening in mid-April. This isn't just another food hall - it's the third Atlanta project from Robert Montwaid, the guy who created New York City's legendary Gansevoort Market. He's partnering with the developers of the Vela Park community near East Lake Golf Club to create something special.

The food hall will feature six different chef-driven concepts along with craft cocktails and plenty of outdoor dining space. It's designed to be the central retail hub for the nearly 12-acre Vela Park rental community, but it should draw people from all over the eastside. The location puts it in the sweet spot between Decatur, Candler Park, and East Atlanta Village.

Montwaid knows how to create food destinations that become neighborhood anchors rather than just tourist traps. If he can replicate even half the magic of Gansevoort Market, the eastside is in for a treat. The timing is perfect too, with all the development happening around East Lake and the growing food scene in that area.
stories.imageAlt: Interior rendering of Vela Park Food Works food hall

stories.headline: ICE Agents Mostly Just Standing Around at Atlanta Airport
stories.summary: Federal agents are deployed to Hartsfield-Jackson to help with TSA lines, but travelers say they're not doing much of anything. With 40% of TSA workers calling out, the airport is telling people to expect four-hour waits. The ICE agents aren't doing security screening or immigration enforcement, just providing 'security presence.'
stories.body:
So ICE agents are at the airport now, and if you're wondering what they're actually doing... well, join the club. Travelers are reporting that the agents are mostly just walking around in tactical gear, not helping with security lines or doing much of anything useful. Mayor Dickens assured everyone they won't be doing immigration enforcement, but nobody seems clear on what they will be doing.

Meanwhile, TSA is still a complete mess with 40% of workers calling out and wait times hitting four hours. The airport is basically telling people to plan on spending half their day getting through security. Some travelers are saying the international terminal might be faster, but that's like saying getting punched in the face is better than getting kicked in the stomach.

This whole situation is peak government response. Instead of fixing the actual problem (paying TSA workers), they send in ICE agents to stand around and look official. At least the Reddit threads about alternative routes through security are getting creative. Nothing brings out Atlanta's problem-solving spirit like a completely dysfunctional airport.
stories.imageAlt: ICE agents walking through Hartsfield-Jackson Atlanta International Airport

stories.headline: Decatur's West Howard Avenue Gets Complete Streets Makeover
stories.summary: The nearly decade-long project to transform West Howard Avenue from a drag strip into a people-friendly street is almost done. The one-mile stretch now has a two-way cycle track, better sidewalks, and traffic calming from Commerce Drive to Paden Circle near East Lake MARTA.
stories.body:
After nearly a decade of planning and construction that started last August, Decatur's West Howard Avenue Complete Streets project is finally wrapping up. The one-mile stretch between downtown Decatur and Atlanta's city limits has been transformed from a high-speed car sewer into something that actually works for people walking and biking.

The project includes a two-way cycle track with concrete barriers, improved sidewalks, and traffic calming measures from Commerce Drive (near Decatur High School) all the way to Paden Circle at East Lake MARTA station. This is exactly the kind of infrastructure investment that makes neighborhoods more livable and connected. Instead of just being a cut-through for commuters, West Howard can actually serve the people who live there.

Decatur has always been ahead of the curve on walkable development, and this project shows they're serious about connecting to the broader regional network. Having protected bike infrastructure that links downtown Decatur to East Lake MARTA is huge for car-free transportation options. It's also a good example of how Complete Streets projects can happen even on busy corridors when there's political will to make it work.
stories.imageAlt: New cycle track with concrete barriers on West Howard Avenue in Decatur

stories.headline: City Council Advances ICE Restrictions While Fire Union Drama Continues
stories.summary: The Public Safety Committee unanimously passed resolutions banning ICE detention facilities in Atlanta and requiring police to document when ICE is present during operations. Meanwhile, firefighters are still mad at Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign their union contract that council already ratified.
stories.body:
Atlanta City Council is making moves on immigration policy while the mayor continues his weird standoff with the fire union. The Public Safety Committee unanimously advanced two resolutions: one banning ICE detention facilities within city limits and another requiring APD to document whenever ICE agents are present during their operations. Both go to the full council for a vote on April 20.

Meanwhile, dozens of firefighters showed up to criticize Mayor Dickens for refusing to sign the city's first-ever contract with the firefighters union. Council already ratified this thing last April, but Dickens is apparently still holding out for reasons that nobody seems to understand. It's a weird hill to die on, especially when you're trying to maintain support from labor.

The ICE restrictions are smart policy that clarifies Atlanta's position without being purely symbolic. Requiring documentation when ICE is involved in local operations gives the city actual data about federal immigration enforcement. And frankly, after seeing ICE agents wandering around the airport doing nothing useful, maybe we need more accountability about when and how federal agents operate in the city.
stories.imageAlt: Atlanta City Council meeting with firefighters in audience

stories.headline: World Cup Prep Displaces Black Residents in Name of Climate Action
stories.summary: As Atlanta spends billions preparing for World Cup semifinals at Mercedes-Benz Stadium, flood control projects are pushing out longtime Black residents from nearby neighborhoods. The city's climate resilience efforts are creating a new form of gentrification that activists are calling 'climate gentrification.'
stories.body:
Atlanta's World Cup preparations are creating a painful irony: the city's climate resilience projects are displacing the very communities they're supposed to protect. While billions pour into entertainment districts and transit upgrades to impress international visitors, longtime Black residents in neighborhoods near Mercedes-Benz Stadium are being pushed out by flood control projects.

The story playing out around Rodney Cook Sr. Park is becoming all too familiar. What gets sold as climate adaptation and neighborhood improvement ends up pricing out the people who've lived there for generations. It's a new twist on gentrification that activists are calling 'climate gentrification' - using environmental improvements as a cover for displacement.

This is the challenge of climate resilience in a city with Atlanta's history of racial and economic inequality. The infrastructure improvements are genuinely needed, but who benefits and who bears the costs? As we get ready to host the world, we need to make sure we're not sacrificing the communities that make Atlanta special in the first place.
stories.imageAlt: View of downtown Atlanta from Rodney Cook Sr. Park showing flood control infrastructure

stories.headline: Classic Oakhurst Craftsman Gets Modern Family-Friendly Makeover
stories.summary: A design-minded couple with two kids worked with Alair Homes to transform their century-old Craftsman in Oakhurst. They kept the curb appeal and character while creating spaces that work for modern family life and entertaining. The renovation was inspired by their impressive art collection.
stories.body:
Oakhurst continues to charm with its front-porch community and Porchfest vibes, and one Craftsman just got a thoughtful renovation that shows how to update these century-old homes without losing their soul. A couple with two school-aged sons worked with builder Heather Shuster of Alair Homes to transform their house into something that works for how families actually live today.

The key was keeping all the character and curb appeal that makes Oakhurst special while creating interior spaces that support modern family life and entertaining. The couple's impressive art collection became a driving force behind many of the design decisions, creating a home that feels both rooted in the neighborhood's history and perfectly suited to contemporary life.

This is exactly the kind of renovation Oakhurst needs more of - thoughtful updates that respect the neighborhood's character while making these old houses work for modern families. It's a good model for how to invest in historic neighborhoods without gentrifying them beyond recognition.
stories.imageAlt: Modern interior of renovated Craftsman home in Oakhurst

stories.headline: Eight-Foot Python Shows Up on the Beltline
stories.summary: Someone spotted and filmed an 8-foot python being handled by a mystery man on the Atlanta Beltline. Wildlife officials are warning people not to handle unknown snakes, and nobody knows if the guy in the video was the owner or just some random uncle who decided to wrangle a giant snake.
stories.body:
Just when you thought the Beltline couldn't get any weirder, someone captured video of an 8-foot python and a mystery man who apparently decided to just grab it and yank it out of the bushes. Joseph Ragland filmed the whole thing and posted it, noting that 'somebody's uncle comes out of nowhere and starts yanking it out of the bush.' Classic Atlanta energy right there.

Wildlife experts are appropriately concerned, both about the snake being loose and about random people handling potentially dangerous reptiles. The Southeastern Reptile Rescue folks say this is exactly what you shouldn't do when you encounter a large snake of unknown origin. But hey, this is Atlanta, where someone's always ready to handle a situation whether they're qualified or not.

The real question is whether this was an escaped pet or someone's exotic animal that got loose. Either way, it's a reminder that the Beltline truly has everything: joggers, cyclists, food trucks, and apparently large pythons. Just another day in the city too busy to hate but not too busy to wrangle snakes.
stories.imageAlt: Large python on the Atlanta Beltline being handled by an unidentified man
